userStories.title: Add stopWhen to PRD for declarative loop termination conditions
userStories.description: Add a `stopWhen` field to prd.json that lets users define custom conditions for when the agent loop should stop, beyond the existing all-stories-complete and max-iterations defaults. Support: stories (stop when specific story IDs complete), maxCostUsd (stop when accumulated cost exceeds threshold, ACP only), maxDurationMinutes (stop when session time exceeds limit). Conditions use OR logic — any condition being met triggers a stop. Also add a file deletion guard that detects and logs when the agent tries to delete files outside the project directory.
acceptanceCriteria: StopWhenCondition interface added to types.ts with stories, maxCostUsd, maxDurationMinutes fields
PRD interface extended with optional stopWhen field
prd.schema.json updated with stopWhen property definition
PRDManager.shouldStop() evaluates conditions with OR logic
Iterator checks stopWhen after each iteration (after stories check, before maxStories check)
File deletion guard: detect tool calls that delete files outside allowed roots, log warning to .agent-output.log and progress.log
isPathSafe() helper in file-utils.ts for path boundary checks
TEMPLATE_PRD in init.ts includes stopWhen example (empty/commented)
Backward compatible: missing stopWhen keeps current behavior
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Inspired by Vercel AI SDK's stopWhen callback. Since prd.json is static JSON, conditions are declarative (not callbacks). File deletion guard is safety-first: log but don't block initially, can add blocking later. Example stopWhen: { "stories": ["US-001"], "maxCostUsd": 5.0, "maxDurationMinutes": 30 }

userStories.title: Research and adopt AI SDK loop control patterns
userStories.description: Review the Vercel AI SDK's loop control primitives (maxSteps, stopWhen, onStepFinish, tool execution patterns) and evaluate which concepts can improve agent-cli's iteration loop. Focus on: conditional termination, step-level hooks, error recovery strategies, and structured progress reporting.
acceptanceCriteria: Document which AI SDK patterns apply to agent-cli's architecture
Identify gaps where agent-cli can improve beyond current patterns
Evaluate onStepFinish for potential per-iteration hooks (e.g., onIterationFinish callback)
Compare error recovery: AI SDK retry patterns vs agent-cli's current approach
List actionable improvements as future stories
userStories.notes: Use /playwright-cli skill in headed mode to browse the AI SDK documentation. Navigate to https://ai-sdk.dev/docs/agents/loop-control and read the pages on maxSteps, stopWhen, onStepFinish, tool execution patterns, and structured output. Then visit related pages linked from there (multi-step reasoning, error handling). All research must be done via the browser — do not use web search or fetch tools. Write findings to .tmp/research/ai-sdk-loop-control.md with sections for each pattern, applicability to agent-cli, and actionable improvements.

userStories.title: Design and implement a session state reducer
userStories.description: Create a reducer pattern to manage session state across iterations. Currently state is scattered: session file (.session.json), PRD manager, iterator fields (iterationCount, storiesCompletedThisRun, acpTotalCost, acpTotalDuration, sessionCompletedIds). A reducer centralizes state transitions (story completed, iteration finished, session interrupted, branch changed, stopWhen triggered) into a single predictable state machine with an immutable state object. This makes state persistence, resumption, and observability straightforward — dispatch an action, get next state, persist it.
acceptanceCriteria: Define SessionState interface with all current state fields (iteration, completed stories, cost, duration, branch, ACP session ID, stopWhen status)
Define action types: STORY_COMPLETED, ITERATION_FINISHED, SESSION_INTERRUPTED, BRANCH_CHANGED, STOPWHEN_TRIGGERED, SESSION_RESUMED
Reducer function: (state, action) => nextState — pure, no side effects
Replace scattered state mutations in iterator.ts with dispatch calls
State persisted to .session.json after every dispatch (single write point)
Monitor/observability modules read from reducer state instead of aggregating from multiple sources
Backward compatible: existing .session.json files load into initial state
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Inspired by Redux-like reducer pattern and AI SDK's internal state management. Current pain points: (1) session state updated in 4+ places in iterator.ts, (2) cost/duration tracked as instance fields separate from session file, (3) resumption reconstructs state from multiple sources. A reducer solves all three. Future: could enable undo/redo, time-travel debugging, and state snapshots for the monitor TUI.

userStories.title: Fix --story flag: inject target into prompt and accept case-insensitive IDs
userStories.description: Two bugs with --story: (1) The target story is never communicated to the agent — the prompt file tells the agent to pick the highest-priority story, so --story US-070 still executes US-068 (priority 4). The fix: when --story is set, append a directive to the prompt telling the agent which story to work on. (2) ID matching is case-sensitive — us-069 should match US-069.
acceptanceCriteria: When --story is set, the prompt sent to the agent (both legacy stdin pipe and ACP sendPrompt) includes a clear directive to work only on the targeted story
Directive is appended to the prompt content (e.g., 'IMPORTANT: Work ONLY on story US-070 this iteration')
Agent works on the targeted story regardless of priority ordering
Legacy path (tool-runner.ts stdin pipe): inject directive into the piped prompt
ACP path (acp-client.ts sendPrompt): inject directive into the prompt content
--story us-069 matches US-069 (case-insensitive)
validateTargetStories uses case-insensitive lookup
getTargetStory uses case-insensitive lookup
Error messages still show the canonical (original case) ID
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: The prompt injection is the critical fix. Without it, --story only affects iterator tracking (headers, completion checks) but the agent ignores it. Add a buildTargetDirective(storyIds, stories) helper that generates the directive text. Inject it in runLiveIteration (legacy) and runACPIteration (ACP) before sending the prompt.

userStories.title: Add --prompt flag for extra instructions and post-run commands
userStories.description: Add a --prompt <text-or-path> CLI flag that appends extra instructions to the agent's prompt each iteration. Accepts either inline text or a path to a .md file. Detects automatically: if the value ends with .md and the file exists, read its content; otherwise treat as literal text. Multiple --prompt flags supported. Instructions are appended after the prompt file content and --story directive.
acceptanceCriteria: CLI flag --prompt <text-or-path> added to main command
Multiple --prompt flags supported (each appends)
Auto-detect: if value ends with .md and file exists, read file content; otherwise use as literal text
Example text: agent-cli 5 --prompt 'after completing, commit and push'
Example file: agent-cli 5 --prompt ./instructions/post-run-tasks.md
Example mixed: agent-cli 5 --prompt ./ci-checks.md --prompt 'commit after each story'
Prompt content appended after the file content and --story directive
Works in both legacy path (tool-runner stdin) and ACP path (sendPrompt)
Error if specified .md file does not exist
Dry-run mode logs the extra prompt but does not execute it
Backward compatible: omitting --prompt keeps current behavior
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Reuses the same prompt suffix injection pattern from US-071 (buildTargetDirective). Add a config.extraPrompt field (string[]) populated from --prompt flags. Append after the --story directive in both runLegacyIteration and runACPIteration. Common use cases: commit/push after stories, run tests, create PRs, notify via Slack.

userStories.title: Track and report context window usage per iteration and session
userStories.description: Capture token usage (input, output, cache read/write) from the agent's output and report context window utilization at the end of each iteration and when the session finishes. In the legacy path, Claude Code's stream-json format includes usage data in the result event (input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens). In the ACP path, usage events carry the same data. Calculate percentage of context used based on the model's known context window size and report it in the CLI output, progress.log, and .agent-output.log.
acceptanceCriteria: Capture token counts from stream-json result event in legacy path (input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens)
Capture token counts from ACP usage events
Track cumulative tokens across iterations (session total input, output, cache)
Report per-iteration token usage in CLI output and .agent-output.log
Report session summary at end: total tokens, estimated context window % used, cache hit rate
Context window sizes mapped per model (Claude models: 200k, etc.)
Display format: 'Context: 45.2k/200k (22.6%) | Cache hit: 78%'
Token data persisted to .session.json for resume sessions
Backward compatible: if token data unavailable, skip reporting
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Legacy path: the stream-json result event already has total_cost_usd and duration_ms — extend to also capture token fields. ACP path: usage events already emit to the event bus — aggregate them. Known context windows: claude-sonnet-4-6/claude-opus-4-7 = 200k tokens. This helps users understand when they're approaching context limits and plan story granularity accordingly.

userStories.title: Test US-073 context window tracking implementation
userStories.description: Verify that US-073 (context window usage tracking) is working correctly by running agent-cli and checking that token counts are captured and reported in the CLI output, .agent-output.log, and .session.json.
acceptanceCriteria: Run agent-cli 1 --tool claude --story US-073 and verify token counts appear in iteration output
CLI output shows format: 'Context: Xk/200k (Y%) | Cache hit: Z%'
.agent-output.log contains per-iteration token breakdown (input, output, cache read, cache write)
.session.json has totalTokens field with cumulative token data after run
Session summary at end shows total tokens and context % used
Dry-run mode gracefully skips token reporting (no data available)
Run with --resume and verify token data accumulates correctly across sessions
userStories.notes: Run: agent-cli 2 --tool claude --story US-069 --prompt 'report token usage in output'. Then check: .agent-output.log for token lines, .session.json for totalTokens. Also test dry-run: agent-cli 2 --dry-run (should not crash or show zero tokens).
